evals.name: label-quality-rules
evals.prompt:
What's wrong with this VoiceOver label?

```swift
Button(action: toggleFavorite) {
    Image(systemName: "heart.fill")
}
.accessibilityLabel("Heart icon button")
```

assertions.text: Identifies that the label should not include the element type ('button')
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 11: VoiceOver adds the role from traits

assertions.text: Identifies that the label should not describe appearance ('heart icon')
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: labels describe purpose not appearance

assertions.text: Provides corrected label describing the action (e.g., 'Add to favorites' or 'Favorite')
assertions.rationale: WITH gives purpose-based label; WITHOUT often keeps appearance description

evals.name: product-card-grouping

assertions.text: Uses .accessibilityElement(children: .combine) or .ignore with custom label
assertions.rationale: Skill's grouping decision tree for product cards

assertions.text: Hides individual star images from VoiceOver (accessibilityHidden or ignoring children)
assertions.rationale: Star images should not each be separate VoiceOver stops

assertions.text: Provides a natural-language rating description (e.g., '4 out of 5 stars')

evals.name: list-cell-custom-actions

assertions.text: Uses accessibilityCustomActions to consolidate multiple buttons into swipe-up/down actions
assertions.rationale: Skill's custom actions section solves repeated button lists

assertions.text: Groups the cell with .accessibilityElement(children:) to create one VoiceOver stop per cell
assertions.rationale: WITHOUT keeps buttons as separate stops

assertions.text: Explains that users swipe up/down to cycle through custom actions
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the VoiceOver interaction pattern

assertions.text: Each custom action closure returns a Bool (true for success)

evals.name: header-trait-rotor
evals.prompt: How do I make VoiceOver announce a section heading?

assertions.text: Uses .accessibilityAddTraits(.isHeader)
assertions.rationale: Skill's critical rule: always add .isHeader on section headings

assertions.text: Mentions that .isHeader enables rotor heading navigation
assertions.rationale: Skill explains WHY headers matter — rotor navigation

evals.name: trait-assignment-destruction
evals.prompt:
I have a custom toggle in UIKit. When I set it as selected, VoiceOver stops saying 'button':

```swift
myToggle.accessibilityTraits = .selected
```

assertions.text: Identifies that assignment (=) destroys existing traits like .button
assertions.rationale: Skill's AI failure F10: assigning traits instead of inserting

assertions.text: Recommends .insert(.selected) instead of = .selected
Shows the correct code with .accessibilityTraits.insert(.selected)
assertions.rationale: WITHOUT may suggest fix but miss insert vs assign

evals.name: adjustable-rating-stepper

assertions.text: Replaces onTapGesture with Button or uses accessibilityAdjustableAction
Adds .accessibilityAdjustableAction with increment/decrement cases
Adds .accessibilityValue with current rating as human-readable text
assertions.rationale: Skill requires value for stateful controls

assertions.text: Groups stars into a single accessibility element with .accessibilityElement()
assertions.rationale: Individual stars should not be separate VoiceOver stops

evals.name: combine-vs-ignore
evals.prompt: What's the difference between .accessibilityElement(children: .combine) and .ignore?

assertions.text: Explains .combine merges children labels with pauses between them
Explains .ignore hides children and requires a custom label on parent
Recommends .ignore + custom label when natural sentence reading is needed
assertions.rationale: Skill's decision tree for natural sentences

evals.name: table-row-grouping

assertions.text: Groups each row with .accessibilityElement(children: .combine) or .ignore
assertions.rationale: Skill's grouping example uses exact table scenario

assertions.text: Provides a natural label for at least one row (e.g., 'Storage: 5 gigabytes for Basic, 100 gigabytes for Pro')
assertions.rationale: Skill shows .ignore + custom label pattern for table rows

assertions.text: Mentions shouldGroupAccessibilityChildren or accessibilitySortPriority for ordering
assertions.rationale: Skill covers both approaches for navigation order

evals.name: canvas-accessibility

assertions.text: Uses .accessibilityChildren to provide virtual accessible elements for data points
Adds an .accessibilityLabel on the Canvas describing the chart type
Each data point child has its own accessibilityLabel with meaningful description
assertions.rationale: WITHOUT often provides only container label

evals.name: hint-vs-label
evals.prompt: When should I use accessibilityHint vs accessibilityLabel?

assertions.text: States hints describe the RESULT of the action, not the action itself
States hints begin with a verb (third person)
Warns against redundant hints like 'Tap to delete' on a delete button
assertions.rationale: Skill has explicit anti-pattern for redundant hints

evals.name: announcement-notifications
evals.prompt: I need to post a VoiceOver announcement when an async operation completes. What notification should I use?

assertions.text: Uses AccessibilityNotification.Announcement (iOS 17+) or UIAccessibility.post(notification: .announcement)
assertions.rationale: Skill documents both SwiftUI and UIKit APIs

assertions.text: Distinguishes .announcement (no focus change) from .layoutChanged and .screenChanged
assertions.rationale: Skill's notification table distinguishes all three

assertions.text: Mentions that announcements can be lost if VoiceOver is already speaking

evals.name: modal-focus-trapping

assertions.text: Sets accessibilityViewIsModal = true on the modal view
Warns that accessibilityViewIsModal only hides SIBLINGS, not all other views
Mentions view hierarchy matters — modal should be direct child of container
Recommends implementing accessibilityPerformEscape for dismiss gesture

evals.name: hardcoded-font-fix
evals.prompt:
What's wrong with this font?

```swift
Text("Welcome").font(.system(size: 24))
```

assertions.text: Identifies hardcoded font size as an accessibility issue
Recommends a specific Dynamic Type text style replacement (e.g., .title)
assertions.rationale: Skill provides text style mapping table

assertions.text: Mentions that 25%+ of iOS users change their text size

evals.name: uifontmetrics-custom-font
evals.prompt:
I use a custom font (Avenir) in UIKit. How do I make it respect Dynamic Type?

```swift
label.font = UIFont(name: "Avenir-Medium", size: 17)
```

assertions.text: Uses UIFontMetrics(forTextStyle:).scaledFont(for:)
Sets adjustsFontForContentSizeCategory = true
assertions.rationale: Skill marks this as CRITICAL for live updating

assertions.text: Sets numberOfLines = 0 to allow text wrapping

evals.name: layout-adaptation-accessibility-sizes
evals.prompt:
My horizontal layout breaks when users set accessibility text sizes. The HStack overflows and clips:

```swift
HStack {
    Image(systemName: "person.fill")
    Text(user.name).font(.headline)
    Spacer()
    Text(user.role).font(.subheadline)
}
```

assertions.text: Checks dynamicTypeSize.isAccessibilitySize to switch layout
Uses ViewThatFits or AnyLayout to switch HStack to VStack
Uses @ScaledMetric for the image/icon size so it scales with Dynamic Type
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 14: non-text dimensions must use @ScaledMetric

assertions.text: Wraps content in ScrollView for overflow at large sizes
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 14: wrap in ScrollView for overflow

evals.name: hardcoded-colors-dark-mode
evals.prompt:
What's the accessibility problem here?

```swift
Text("Hello").foregroundColor(.black)
    .background(Color.white)
```

assertions.text: Identifies hardcoded colors as dark mode failure
Recommends .foregroundStyle(.primary) and Color(.systemBackground)
Mentions that .foregroundColor is deprecated, use .foregroundStyle
assertions.rationale: Skill's AI failure F4: deprecated API

evals.name: color-only-status-indicator
evals.prompt:
I'm using color to show online/offline status. A colorblind user reported they can't tell the difference:

```swift
Circle().fill(isOnline ? .green : .red)
    .frame(width: 12, height: 12)
```

assertions.text: Identifies color-only indicator as accessibility failure (WCAG 1.4.1)
Adds shape and/or text alongside color (checkmark/xmark icons or text labels)
assertions.rationale: Skill shows color + shape + text pattern

assertions.text: Mentions accessibilityDifferentiateWithoutColor environment value

evals.name: wcag-contrast-calculation
evals.prompt: I need to verify my custom color scheme meets WCAG contrast requirements. My text color is #6B7280 on a #F9FAFB background. Does it pass?

assertions.text: States WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text
assertions.rationale: Skill documents exact WCAG contrast ratios

assertions.text: Calculates or estimates the contrast ratio for the given colors
Mentions testing contrast independently for both light AND dark modes
assertions.rationale: Skill requires separate contrast testing per mode

assertions.text: Mentions Increase Contrast setting (colorSchemeContrast) may need 7:1
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 15: check all system preferences including contrast

evals.name: reduce-motion-check
evals.prompt: How do I check if the user has Reduce Motion enabled?

assertions.text: Shows @Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion) for SwiftUI
assertions.rationale: Skill documents both SwiftUI and UIKit approaches

assertions.text: Recommends replacing motion with crossfade/opacity, not removing all animation
assertions.rationale: Skill: 'Don't remove ALL animation — replace with subtle crossfade'

evals.name: spring-animation-reduce-motion

assertions.text: Checks reduceMotion environment value before applying spring animation
Provides alternative animation for reduce motion (e.g., .none or .easeInOut)
Shows the ternary pattern: reduceMotion ? .none : .spring()

evals.name: all-system-preferences
evals.prompt: What system accessibility preferences should I check beyond Reduce Motion? I want to build a truly adaptive UI.

assertions.text: Lists reduceTransparency with replacement pattern (solid bg vs .ultraThinMaterial)
Lists differentiateWithoutColor for color-only indicators
Lists legibilityWeight (.bold) for bold text preference
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 15: check ALL system preferences including legibilityWeight

assertions.text: Lists colorSchemeContrast for Increase Contrast preference
Mentions accessibilityIgnoresInvertColors for photos/videos

evals.name: ontapgesture-invisible
evals.prompt:
What's the accessibility issue with this?

```swift
Image(systemName: "plus")
    .onTapGesture { addItem() }
```

assertions.text: Identifies onTapGesture as invisible to VoiceOver and assistive technology
Replaces with Button and adds accessibilityLabel
assertions.rationale: Skill's critical rule: always use Button

assertions.text: Mentions Switch Control and/or eye tracking as also affected
assertions.rationale: Skill documents that ALL assistive tech is affected

evals.name: custom-toggle-accessible

assertions.text: Replaces onTapGesture with Button or native Toggle
Adds .accessibilityAddTraits(.isToggle) or uses native Toggle for toggle semantics
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 13: use .isToggle on custom toggle-like controls

assertions.text: Adds .accessibilityValue with current state ('On'/'Off')
assertions.rationale: Skill requires value for stateful controls

assertions.text: Groups the control elements or uses .accessibilityElement(children:)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 13: group toggle label and visual indicator

evals.name: form-label-association

assertions.text: Uses .accessibilityLabel on each TextField to explicitly associate the label text
Uses .accessibilityLabeledPair or explicit accessibilityLabel for cross-hierarchy association
Adds .textContentType (.username, .emailAddress, .password) for autofill

evals.name: identifier-vs-label
evals.prompt:
My button's accessibilityIdentifier is set but VoiceOver doesn't read anything useful. Why?

```swift
button.accessibilityIdentifier = "favoriteButton"
```

assertions.text: Explains accessibilityIdentifier is for UI testing only, not read by VoiceOver
Recommends setting accessibilityLabel for VoiceOver
Shows both properties set independently (label for users, identifier for tests)

evals.name: disabled-controls-visibility
evals.prompt:
My disabled submit button disappears from VoiceOver. Users don't know it exists:

```swift
submitButton.isEnabled = false
submitButton.isAccessibilityElement = false
```

assertions.text: Identifies isAccessibilityElement = false as the problem
Recommends .notEnabled trait: VoiceOver reads 'dimmed'
assertions.rationale: Skill's rule: disabled = dimmed, not hidden

assertions.text: Explains WHY 'dimmed' matters — user knows control exists
assertions.rationale: Skill explains the UX of hearing 'Submit, dimmed'

evals.name: custom-drawn-graph-uikit

assertions.text: Sets isAccessibilityElement = false on the container
Overrides accessibilityElements to return UIAccessibilityElement array
Sets accessibilityFrame using UIAccessibility.convertToScreenCoordinates
Each element has accessibilityLabel and accessibilityValue
assertions.rationale: WITHOUT often provides only container label

evals.name: wcag-text-resize-criterion
evals.prompt: What WCAG criterion covers text resizing on iOS?

assertions.text: References WCAG 1.4.4 (Resize Text)
Maps to iOS API: preferredFont(forTextStyle:) or Dynamic Type styles
assertions.rationale: Skill provides specific iOS API per criterion

evals.name: drag-and-drop-accessibility
evals.prompt: My app has drag-and-drop for reordering items. A user with motor impairment can't use it. What WCAG criteria apply and how do I fix it for iOS?

assertions.text: References WCAG 2.5.7 (Dragging Movements) — new in WCAG 2.2
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 16 and WCAG 2.2 new criteria

assertions.text: Recommends single-tap alternative using accessibilityCustomActions
Mentions minimum target size (24x24pt WCAG 2.5.8, or 44x44pt Apple HIG)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 16: ensure minimum touch target size

evals.name: wcag-auto-vs-manual
evals.prompt: Which WCAG 2.2 criteria are automatically handled by standard UIKit/SwiftUI controls and which require manual implementation? Give me the top 5 that developers miss most.

assertions.text: Lists at least 3 automatically handled criteria (e.g., 4.1.2, 2.5.2, 2.1.1)
Lists 1.1.1 (Non-text Content) as requiring manual implementation
assertions.rationale: Skill marks this as high-priority AI failure

assertions.text: Lists criteria that DON'T apply to native iOS apps (e.g., 4.1.1 Parsing, 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks)
Identifies at least one WCAG 2.2 NEW criterion (2.5.7, 2.5.8, 3.3.7, or 3.3.8)

evals.name: xctest-accessibility-audit
evals.prompt: How do I run an automated accessibility audit in my XCTest UI tests?

assertions.text: Uses performAccessibilityAudit() (Xcode 15+)
assertions.rationale: Skill documents XCTest accessibility audit API

assertions.text: Shows the audit can be selective with specific types (e.g., .contrast, .dynamicType)

evals.name: teardown-audit-pattern
evals.prompt: I want to add accessibility auditing to every UI test without writing it in each test method. What's the pattern?

assertions.text: Puts performAccessibilityAudit() in tearDown/tearDownWithError
assertions.rationale: Skill's regression pattern: audit in tearDown

assertions.text: Shows how to filter known issues with the closure parameter
Mentions this catches regressions at zero additional test-writing cost

evals.name: voiceover-manual-protocol
evals.prompt: Give me a comprehensive VoiceOver manual testing protocol — what should I check and in what order?

assertions.text: Includes linear navigation test (swipe right through entire screen)
Includes rotor heading navigation test
assertions.rationale: Skill verifies section structure via rotor

assertions.text: Mentions Screen Curtain test (triple three-finger tap) as gold standard
Tests modal focus trapping (escape gesture dismisses)

evals.name: decorative-image-approaches
evals.prompt: What's the difference between Image(decorative:) and Image("name").accessibilityHidden(true)?

assertions.text: States both hide the image from VoiceOver
Recommends Image(decorative:) as the preferred SwiftUI approach
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 12: Image(decorative:) is the SwiftUI way

evals.name: reduce-transparency-fix
evals.prompt: My app uses blur effects (.ultraThinMaterial) extensively. A user with reduce transparency enabled can't read the content. How do I fix this?

assertions.text: Checks @Environment(\.accessibilityReduceTransparency)
Replaces .ultraThinMaterial with solid Color(.systemBackground) when enabled
Uses AnyShapeStyle or conditional for the SwiftUI type mismatch

evals.name: smart-invert-and-large-content
evals.prompt: I have photos in my app. When Smart Invert Colors is enabled, the photos look inverted and wrong. Also, some users report my toolbar buttons are too small to tap at accessibility text sizes.

assertions.text: Uses accessibilityIgnoresInvertColors = true on photos/images
Mentions setting on parent cascades to subviews
Uses accessibilityShowsLargeContentViewer for toolbar buttons that can't scale
assertions.rationale: Skill's Large Content Viewer for non-scalable controls

assertions.text: Caps Dynamic Type at xxxLarge for toolbar and shows Large Content Viewer as fallback
assertions.rationale: Skill combines .dynamicTypeSize cap with Large Content Viewer
